name: Platform-Neutral Access Framework
consequences: Immediate: Partial decoupling from Apple/Google trust signals. Systemic: 25% faster scaling through parallel certification tracks and diversified vendor on-ramps. Strategic: reduces supplier concentration and enhances sovereign policy leverage and resilience.
options: Maintain current vendor relationships while inserting anti-concentration clauses and staged milestones into contracts, preserving MitID trust anchors while gradually increasing platform neutrality.
Mandate platform-neutral fallback readiness in all new contracts, with phased migrations, cost-tracking dashboards, and performance-based incentives.
Create a government-backed identity utility with open-source components and a subscription-based funding model to decouple from platform vendors.
review: Trade-off: Controls Platform Dependency vs. Implementation Cost and Timeline. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border EU alignment and private-sector incentives.

name: Procurement-Resilience and Standards Leverage
consequences: Immediate: Procurement constraints tightened; Systemic: 25% faster cross-agency onboarding through standardized contracts and shared attestations; Strategic: reduces vendor lock-in yet increases governance overhead and compliance complexity with longer procurement cycles.
options: Retain core supplier relationships while introducing anti-concentration clauses and staged milestones to gradually diversify without forcing immediate architectural shifts.
Mandate platform-neutral fallback readiness in all new contracts, establishing phased migrations, governance dashboards, and performance-based incentives.
Create a government-backed identity utility with a Nordic–EU consortium model and revenue-sharing, decoupling from platform vendors while piloting a pay-for-use attestations marketplace.
review: Trade-off: Controls Purchasing Autonomy vs. Bureaucratic Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider small-supplier viability and volatile funding streams.

name: Regulatory-Policy Coalition Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Policy commitments signed; Systemic: 25% faster standards adoption through formal NSIS/eIDAS2 alignment and procurement language; Strategic: enhances sovereignty though risk of capture by incumbent stakeholders and funding uncertainty ahead.
options: Build a broad coalition and secure non-binding policy commitments, pursuing resilience framing while avoiding binding standards until pilots prove value.
Draft binding NSIS/eIDAS2-aligned standards and procurement language, implement formal impact assessments, and establish a neutral testbed for AltID interoperability.
Pioneer a platform-neutral identity utility with regulatory sandbox and a subscription-based funding model to license AltID interoperability.
review: Trade-off: Controls Regulatory Coherence vs. Speed of Reform. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border harmonization with EU standards and private-sector incentives.

name: Technological Demonstration and Standards Enabler
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrator results reveal platform-agnostic gaps; Systemic: 25% faster knowledge transfer via shared testbeds and cross-agency reuse; Strategic: accelerates interoperability yet risks scope creep and funding drift with governance considerations.
options: Develop non-production demonstrators focused on open standards such as WebAuthn and OpenID Connect within a privacy-respecting GrapheneOS environment.
Develop two robust demonstrators: one mobile-focused and one browser-based fallback, ensuring cross-platform compatibility and certification alignment.
Establish a government-backed incubator to commercialize open standards with a platform-neutral trust anchor and a subscription-based revenue model for ongoing identity services.
review: Trade-off: Controls Demonstration Pace vs. Security Assurance. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs and security testing within a live EU-compatible environment.

name: EU Alignment and International Cooperation
consequences: Immediate: Policy alignment enhances legitimacy; Systemic: 25% faster cross-border adoption through shared standards and agreements; Strategic: increases influence but raises exposure to EU policy cycles and fund dependence and governance.
options: Engage EU bodies at a high level, gather input, and align with European Wallet developments, without heavy funding.
Lead a formal Nordic–EU interoperability forum, draft joint standards proposals, and run cross-border pilots.
Pioneer cross-EU platform-neutral identity alliance with shared trust anchors and EU identity fund to bankroll platform-neutral fallbacks.
review: Trade-off: National Autonomy vs EU-scale Interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider domestic political economy and budget constraints.

name: Open Standards Adoption Framework
consequences: Immediate: Partial decoupling from single-vendor signals via adapters → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through parallel vendor onboarding and shared attestations → Strategic: reduces vendor lock-in and increases sovereignty and procurement leverage. Trade-off: Speed of deployment vs governance overhead.
options: Preserve current MitID dependencies while embedding open-standards adapters in procurement and architecture to enable future soft-switching, with staged milestones and transparent cost accounting.
Mandate platform-neutral fallbacks in new contracts, establish governance dashboards, and pursue cross-agency cross-vendor attestation sharing.

Launch a government-backed platform-neutral identity utility built on open standards, funded by a subscription model and EU grant integration, with tokenized governance and cross-border attestations marketplace.
review: Trade-off: Platform Neutrality vs. Implementation Cost and Timeline. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border EU alignment and private-sector incentives.

name: Adaptive Financing and Risk-Sharing Model
consequences: Immediate: Introduce shared-risk funding arrangements with select partners; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through pooled financing and cross-agency buying power; Strategic: reduces budget volatility but raises governance overhead and potential funding volatility.
options: Preserve existing procurement finance while adding optional platform-neutral adapters with cost-sharing for transitional period.
Establish a formal risk-sharing framework including performance-based funding, cross-ministerial budget pooling, and transparent ROI dashboards.
Create a platform-neutral identity utility funded by a mixed model including subscription revenue, EU-backed guarantees, and a governance token to incentivize contributions.
review: Trade-off: Purchasing Autonomy vs. Bureaucratic Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider small-supplier viability and volatile funding streams.

name: Decentralized Identity Sandbox Approach
consequences: Immediate: Establish internal and partner sandbox environments for cross-platform components and fallback flows → Systemic: 25% faster knowledge transfer through shared testbeds and reproducible results across pilots → Strategic: increases interoperability while risk of fragmentation if governance is not harmonized.
options: Develop an internal sandbox environment to test adapters without external dependencies.
Open the sandbox to select partners to evaluate cross-platform components and fallback flows, ensuring alignment with WebAuthn/OpenID Connect and GrapheneOS.
Launch a decentralized identity sandbox using self-sovereign identity with distributed ledger-backed credentials and tokenized governance.
review: Trade-off: Experimentation Speed vs. Security Assurance. Weakness: The options fail to consider EU wallet interoperability alignment.

name: Public-Private Pilot Consortium Model
consequences: Immediate: Forming cross-sector partnerships accelerates learning → Systemic: 25% faster cross-agency evaluation through shared guidelines and procurement language → Strategic: fosters collaboration but risks governance fragmentation and slower decision rights.
options: Form a small cross-agency working group to study procurement language and pilot framework.
Establish a formal public-private pilot consortium with shared governance and cross-border evaluation across agencies.
Create a Nordic-EU platform-neutral identity consortium with shared trust anchors, revenue-sharing, and a governance token to align incentives.
review: Trade-off: Consortium Flexibility vs Governance Fragmentation. Weakness: The options fail to consider alignment with national procurement rules.

name: Dynamic Policy Impact Assessment and Feedback Loop
consequences: Immediate: Implement continuous policy-impact monitoring → Systemic: 25% faster adaptation to changing regulations via dashboards and scenario planning → Strategic: improves agility but increases data governance risk and automation dependency.
options: Install quarterly policy impact reviews using existing compliance checklists and NSIS/eIDAS2 alignment.
Develop dashboards for continuous impact assessment and scenario planning across agencies.
Deploy a real-time AI-assisted policy simulation engine that models cross-border identity outcomes and uses a governance token-based mechanism to adjust standards quickly.
review: Trade-off: Regulatory speed vs. governance quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider domestic political economy and budget constraints.

name: Resilient Identity Governance Framework
consequences: Immediate: Partial decoupling from platform trust signals → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through parallel certification tracks and shared risk pools → Strategic: reduces supplier concentration and strengthens sovereignty. Trade-off: Platform Dependency vs Governance Overhead.
options: Maintain MitID anchors while instituting governance overlays, continuity credits, and risk-based SLAs to ensure service during platform changes.
Adopt platform-neutral fallback readiness with two-vendor migration, shared risk pools, and performance dashboards.
Create a national self-sovereign identity utility built on blockchain-backed attestations, DAO governance, and cross-border attestation marketplace funded by subscription.
review: Trade-off: Controls Platform Dependency vs Governance Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider EU-alignment and private-sector incentives.

name: Open-Source Identity Stack Catalysis
consequences: Immediate: Reduces vendor lock-in by enabling alternative trust anchors → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through shared components → Strategic: accelerates sovereignty but increases governance overhead. Trade-off: Open-source Adoption vs Security/Certification Burden.
options: Fund open-source adapters for WebAuthn/OpenID within existing MitID architecture with government sponsorship and transparent cost accounting.
Mandate open-source components in all future identity initiatives and establish a shared CI/CD and attestation registry.
Launch a community-driven SSI stack built on distributed ledger technology with tokenized governance and cross-border attestations.
review: Trade-off: Open-source adoption vs security/certification burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider time-to-market and regulatory capture risk.

name: Cross-Border Attestation Market and Shared Risk
consequences: Immediate: Faster cross-border interoperability → Systemic: 25% faster scaling via shared attestations and procurement on-ramps → Strategic: reduces vendor lock-in but raises regulatory compliance burdens. Trade-off: Cross-border Interoperability vs Domestic procurement complexity.
options: Establish limited cross-agency attestation sharing with two-way interoperability proofs and limited scope.
Create an EU-backed attestation marketplace with shared funding and governance; allow cross-border pilots.
Establish a cross-border federation using a blockchain-based attestation ledger and a DAO for governance.
review: Trade-off: Controls Cross-Border Interoperability vs Domestic procurement complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider small-vendor viability and regulatory constraints.

name: Decoupled Experience and Access Channels
consequences: Immediate: Improved user access continuity → Systemic: 25% faster scaling via cross-channel testing → Strategic: Increases resilience but raises data-privacy risk and hardware supply risk. Trade-off: User Experience vs Security/Privacy.
options: Develop multi-channel access with offline fallback to hardware tokens and QR-based provisioning.
Pilot browser-based and hardware-token fallback path that reduces platform dependence while preserving user experience.
Implement a global privacy-preserving AI-assisted identity verification with edge computing and secure enclaves, plus a platform-neutral trust anchor.
review: Trade-off: Controls User Experience vs Security/Privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs in EU environments.

name: EU Wallet Readiness and Procurement Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Policy alignment with EU wallet → Systemic: 25% faster cross-border adoption via shared standards → Strategic: Increases legitimacy but raises policy-capture risk. Trade-off: National Autonomy vs EU-scale Interoperability.
options: Map EU wallet requirements and align procurement language with NSIS/eIDAS2; run pilot standards.
Lead a Nordic-EU interoperability forum with shared guidelines and cross-border pilots.
Establish a Europe-wide platform-neutral identity alliance with shared trust anchors, tokenized governance, and cross-border attestation network.
review: Trade-off: National Autonomy vs EU-scale Interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider budget constraints and domestic political economy.
